mappings.rationaleAI: The destruction of Mycenaean Greece, Minoan Crete, and Aegean civilizations c. 1200 BC directly precipitated the Greek Dark Ages and Dorian invasions (B.1.a), while the broader collapse of interconnected Bronze Age palatial economies illustrates the end of the Bronze Age cultural world referenced in A.2.b.

Etruscan civilization from c. 800 BC in Tuscany, including their origins, art, religion, maritime expansion, relations with Greeks and other Italic peoples, and eventual Roman conquest, maps directly onto A.3.a.i. Their formative influence on Roman institutions, writing, and art corresponds to D.2.b.

The Druids as the priestly class of pre-Roman Celtic societies in Ireland, Britain, and Gaul, including their role in Iron Age religion, ritual practice, and social organization, directly addresses Celtic religion and social organization under A.3.b.i.

Archaeological evidence from Scythian burial sites suggesting a historical basis for the Amazon legend in nomadic steppe warrior cultures addresses Scythian society and culture (A.3.c.ii), while their prominence in Greek pottery, sculpture, and literature connects to arts in the Archaic Period (B.1.c.vii).

The 480 BC naval engagement where Greek city-states defeated Xerxes' Persian fleet is a pivotal event in the Greco-Persian Wars (B.1.c.viii), with Themistocles' strategy and the consequences for Greek independence and subsequent classical flourishing directly addressed.

Herodotus' Histories as an inquiry into the origins and course of the Persian Wars is the primary ancient account of the Greco-Persian conflict; the outline at B.1.c.viii explicitly references 'Herodotus' account of the conflict.'

Persepolis as the Achaemenid ceremonial capital destroyed by Alexander in 330 BC, reportedly retaliating for Xerxes' burning of the Athenian Acropolis, connects the Greco-Persian Wars (B.1.c.viii) with Alexander's conquest of the Persian Empire (B.2.h).

Spartan dominance over the Peloponnese, its military-oriented polity and repression of Helots map to B.1.c.ii, while the Peloponnesian League (B.2.b), Spartan role in the Peloponnesian War (B.2.c), and the era of Spartan hegemony (B.2.d) are all substantively addressed.

Pericles' strengthening of democratic institutions, expansion of Athenian imperial power, patronage of the Parthenon, and the funeral oration recorded by Thucydides correspond precisely to Athens in the age of Pericles (B.2.a), covering the Athenian Empire (B.2.a.i), democracy (B.2.a.ii), and cultural efflorescence including the Acropolis rebuilding (B.2.a.iii).
Thucydides' History of the Peloponnesian War is the definitive ancient account of the Athens-Sparta conflict (431-404 BC); the outline at B.2.c explicitly references 'Thucydides' account of the war.'

Aristotle's observation and classification of 4th-century Greek city-state governments, composed in the context of Philip of Macedon's rise, represents a major development in Greek philosophy and political thought in the 4th century BC (B.2.g).

Alexander's conquest of the Persian Empire, Bactria, and the Indus Valley (334-323 BC) and the spread of Hellenism directly correspond to B.2.h, while his death in 323 BC triggered the warfare among rival generals that established the Hellenistic kingdoms (C.1.a).

The Maccabean revolt against Seleucid Hellenization directly addresses Jewish resistance within the Seleucid Kingdom (C.1.c), which the outline explicitly identifies as a feature of the Seleucid realm along with dynastic politics and territorial losses.

Stoicism, founded by Zeno in Athens c. 300 BC, dominated Hellenistic intellectual life (C.3.b) and continued through Roman practitioners Seneca, Epictetus, and Marcus Aurelius into the imperial period (E.3.b), spanning philosophy across both the Hellenistic and Roman eras.

A broad treatment of Rome from mythical origins through the Republic, Empire, and its transformation, covering civic institutions, imperial expansion, the Pax Romana, military organization, and legal systems, provides an overarching narrative spanning the rise of Rome (D) and the Roman Empire (E).

The founding of the Republic after expulsion of King Tarquin (D.2.a), development of constitutional government balancing consuls, Senate, and popular assemblies (D.3.a), expansion to Mediterranean mastery (D.4), and eventual collapse into civil war and dictatorship (D.5) are all substantively covered.

Hannibal's crossing of the Alps, devastating victories at Trebia, Lake Trasimene, and Cannae, and ultimate defeat by Scipio Africanus at Zama correspond directly to the Second Punic War (D.4.a.iii), including the initial Carthaginian victories and the Roman counteroffensive in Africa.

Tiberius and Gaius Gracchus' land redistribution reforms, their violent suppression in 133 and 121 BC, and the introduction of political violence destabilizing the Republic map precisely onto D.5.a (social and economic ills, the reform movement of the Gracchi and its results).

Cicero's consulship and suppression of Catiline's conspiracy (D.5.d.ii), his role in the crisis surrounding Caesar's dictatorship and assassination (D.5.e), and his contributions to oratory and philosophy (D.5.h) are substantively addressed.

Caesar's conquest of Gaul, his political maneuvering leading to the Civil War, his dictatorship, and assassination in 44 BC correspond directly to D.5.e (the alliance of Caesar, Pompey, and Crassus; Caesar's conquest of Gaul; triumph, dictatorship, and assassination).

The economic centrality of slave labor, legal status under Roman law, manumission practices, and slave revolts including Spartacus address Roman government and economy in the middle republic (D.4.c) and the society and economy of the empire at its height (E.3.a).

Augustus' establishment of the principate, social and moral legislation, and the golden age of Latin literature including Virgil, Horace, and Ovid map directly to E.1.a (Augustus' establishment of the principate) and E.1.d (culture of the Augustan Age).

mappings.episodeTitle: Tacitus and the Decadence of Rome
mappings.rationaleAI: Tacitus' Annals and Histories chronicling the Julio-Claudian and Flavian dynasties, with vivid portrayals of emperors Nero and Caligula, correspond to E.1.e (Tacitus' accounts of the Julio-Claudians explicitly referenced in the outline) and E.2.a (the Flavian emperors).

Tiberius' succession from Augustus, his consolidation of imperial institutions, and the divergent assessments of his reign as either competent administration or cruel tyranny directly address the empire under Tiberius (E.1.e).

The Claudian invasion of AD 43 (E.1.e, annexation of Britain), Roman military infrastructure, urbanization, and cultural assimilation of Celtic populations (E.3.a), and the eventual withdrawal c. AD 410 amid Germanic invasions (E.4.g) are all substantively covered.

Josephus' account of the First Jewish-Roman War and the destruction of Jerusalem in 70 AD under Titus directly addresses the Flavian period and the suppression of the Jewish revolt referenced in E.2.a.

Pliny's correspondence during the reigns of Domitian, Nerva, and Trajan provides detailed insights into Roman daily life, provincial governance, and legal practice spanning the Flavian (E.2.a) and Antonine (E.2.b) periods, illuminating the society and administration of the empire at its zenith (E.3.a).

Built under Emperor Hadrian c. AD 122, the Wall exemplifies Roman frontier policy, military engineering, and imperial administration at the empire's northwestern boundary (E.2.b), with the subsequent Antonine Wall illustrating shifting frontier strategies under the Antonine emperors (E.3.a).

Marcus Aurelius as the last of the Five Good Emperors (E.2.b) and his Stoic Meditations composed during Danube frontier campaigns represent both Antonine imperial governance and Greco-Roman philosophical culture of the 2nd century (E.3.b), with his death in 180 AD marking the beginning of imperial decline.

Zenobia's Palmyrene rebellion against Rome and conquest of Egypt during the crisis of the third century, before her defeat by Emperor Aurelian, directly addresses the military anarchy, loss of eastern provinces, and disintegration of the Roman Empire referenced in E.4.d.

The Sassanid Empire as Rome's principal rival from c. 226 AD, including Shapur I's capture of Emperor Valerian, addresses the growth of conflict with Persia under the Severans (E.4.a), while renewed Sassanid wars continued under Constantine's successors (E.4.f.i).

Arianism's trajectory from orthodox theological position to heresy as the eastern Empire committed to the Nicene Creed, and its spread to Gothic peoples who carried it into the western provinces, addresses the rise of Christianity and religious conflict (E.4.b) and the establishment of Christian orthodoxy as state religion (E.4.f.iii).

Constantine's conversion to Christianity, legalization and privileging of the faith, building of Constantinople, and decentralization of the Empire eastward correspond directly to E.4.e.ii (Constantine the Great and his conversion to Christianity, administration, and founding of Constantinople).

Julian's attempt to restore traditional Greco-Roman paganism and reverse Christian institutional dominance after Constantine's conversion, his military campaigns in Gaul, and his death fighting the Sasanian Empire correspond precisely to E.4.f.ii (Julian's reign and the attempt to restore the old empire).
